main_ingredients: glass noodles (mung bean starch cellophane noodles)
allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and bean paste)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Ants Climbing a Tree is approximately 250 g, typically served as a main dish alongside steamed rice at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Glass noodles (mung bean starch) and ground pork composite estimate
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Ants Climbing a Tree exists in USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for mung bean starch glass noodles (FDC ID 168869), ground pork (FDC ID 167903), soybean-based condiments, and cooking oil, weighted to a representative recipe ratio of approximately 50% glass noodles, 25% minced pork, 15% sauce/condiments, and 10% oil and aromatics. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against this composite and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated from pork and oil components. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not reliably derivable from composite and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — mung bean starch noodles (glass noodles / cellophane noodles)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 55 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI and GL Values (Atkinson et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2008) for mung bean starch glass noodles / cellophane noodles, which are the primary carbohydrate component of this dish. The GI of the composite dish is estimated as low because: (1) mung bean starch noodles have a published GI of approximately 39–56 (lower than wheat or rice noodles); (2) the protein and fat from minced pork and cooking oil further attenuate postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (56.25 g from 250 g × 22.5 g/100 g) ÷ 100 = 30.9.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg per 100 g; approximately 1050 mg per standard 250 g serving) from Doubanjiang fermented chili bean paste and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium content; if consumed, portion size should be strictly controlled and no additional salt or soy sauce should be added.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (22.5 g per 100 g) with a low glycemic index (~55) due to mung bean starch noodles; protein and fat from pork further slow glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in controlled portions, as the low GI of mung bean glass noodles is preferable to wheat or rice noodles, but total carbohydrate intake per serving (~56 g) should be factored into the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (420 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (~2.8 g/100 g) from pork and cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this dish due to its high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from minced pork; choosing leaner pork and reducing the amount of Doubanjiang can lower both sodium and saturated fat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory aromatics (ginger, garlic) present; chili may be a trigger for some individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger and garlic in this dish have documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should monitor their response to the chili-based Doubanjiang.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ants Climbing a Tree (蚂蚁上树, Mǎyǐ shàng shù) is a classic Sichuan Chinese dish whose poetic name describes its appearance: small morsels of minced pork ('ants') clinging to translucent glass noodles ('trees'). The dish is made by stir-frying seasoned minced pork with ginger, garlic, and scallions, then incorporating Doubanjiang — a fermented Sichuan chili bean paste — which imparts a deep red colour, umami richness, and characteristic spicy-savoury heat. Soaked mung bean starch cellophane noodles are added to the wok and braised in the seasoned liquid until they become soft, slightly chewy, and fully saturated with flavour. Per 100 g the dish provides approximately 185 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 8.5 g fat, and 22.5 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low), owing to the resistant starch properties of mung bean starch noodles, which produce a slower postprandial glucose response than wheat or rice noodles. Sodium content is high at 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1050 mg per standard 250 g serving), making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy allergens from the bean paste and soy sauce, and gluten from fermented condiments. It is a quick, economical, and deeply flavourful weekday meal that exemplifies the Sichuan culinary philosophy of bold aromatics, layered textures, and the balance of spice, salt, and umami.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Ants Climbing a Tree exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for mung bean glass noodles and ground pork, cross-checked against the AI-generated input values which were internally consistent. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database / AJCN International Tables for mung bean starch noodles as the primary carbohydrate component. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile and moisture data for this composite Sichuan dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as a single entry; composite estimation of these values would carry unacceptable uncertainty. 2. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for ground pork and cooking oil weighted by recipe proportion. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on mung bean starch glass noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database / AJCN International Tables; the composite dish GI may be slightly lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion. 4. Glycemic load per serving = 55 × (250 × 0.225) / 100 = 55 × 56.25 / 100 = 30.9 g. 5. The input portions array contained four identical duplicate entries (all '1 serving (250g)', 250 g); deduplicated to a single 250 g serving. 6. Sodium content is high; this dish is not recommended for individuals on sodium-restricted diets. 7. This dish is not Singaporean; G6 multilingual requirements for Singaporean dishes (Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. Chinese-script names are provided as the dish is Chinese in origin.
